verses.commentary: Jesus leaves Galilee, goes to Judea and across the Jordan. Journey to Jerusalem begins. Crowds come, he teaches. The pattern continues.
Pharisees test him: Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife? Trap question. Hillel and Shammai schools divided on this. Whatever Jesus says will offend someone.
What did Moses command? Jesus deflects, forces them to state the law first.
They say: Moses permitted a certificate of divorce (Deuteronomy 24:1). They cite permission, not command.
Jesus: It was because your hearts were hard that Moses wrote this law. Concession, not ideal. Divorce is accommodation to sinfulness, not God's design.
But at the beginning of creation God made them male and female. Genesis 1:27. Jesus appeals to creation order, not Mosaic law. God's original intent precedes and supersedes later concessions.
A man will leave father and mother, be united to his wife. Genesis 2:24. Permanent relational transfer. New primary bond.
The two become one flesh. No longer two, but one. Ontological unity. Marriage creates a new reality.
What God has joined together, let no one separate. Divine act, human prohibition. Marriage is sacred, indissoluble. Revolutionary teaching.
In the house, disciples ask privately. They need clarification. This teaching is shocking.
Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery against her. Stunning. He calls remarriage adultery. Permanence of the original bond.
If she divorces him and marries another, she commits adultery. Gender equality in judgment. Both bound by the same standard.
People bring children to Jesus for him to touch them. Disciples rebuke them. They see children as interruptions, not important enough for Jesus' attention.

Jesus is indignant. Strong emotion. Let the children come; do not hinder them. The kingdom belongs to such as these. Children are not obstacles but exemplars. Dependent, powerless, trusting. This is kingdom citizenship.
Anyone who does not receive the kingdom like a little child will never enter it. Childlikeness is the entry requirement. Humility, dependence, trust. Not childishness, but childlikeness.
He takes them in his arms, places hands on them, blesses them. Incarnational tenderness. Jesus embraces what disciples dismissed.
A man runs to Jesus, kneels. Good teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life? Eager, respectful. But wrong question. Do versus receive.
Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone. Probing question. Either Jesus is not good, or Jesus is God. The man must face the implication.
You know the commandments. Jesus lists the second table: murder, adultery, theft, false witness, defraud, honor parents. Horizontal relationships.
All these I have kept since I was a boy. Confident claim. External obedience, but is it enough?

Jesus looked at him and loved him. Tender moment. One thing you lack. Go, sell everything, give to the poor, you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me. Jesus exposes the heart. Wealth is his idol. The first commandment is violated: another god before God.
His face fell. He went away sad, because he had great wealth. The only person in the Gospels who walks away from Jesus' call. Riches won. Tragic.
Jesus: How hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom of God! Counter-cultural. Jewish thought equated wealth with divine blessing. Jesus reverses it: wealth is a barrier.
Disciples amazed. Jesus repeats: Children, how hard it is to enter the kingdom! Universal difficulty, but especially for the rich.
Easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for the rich to enter the kingdom. Hyperbolic impossibility. Largest animal, smallest opening. Wealth chokes faith.
Disciples even more amazed: Who then can be saved? If the wealthy (blessed by God, they thought) cannot be saved, who can?
Jesus: With man this is impossible, but not with God; all things are possible with God. Salvation is divine work, not human achievement. Grace breaks the impossible.
Peter: We have left everything to follow you! Implied: What about us? We did what the rich man couldn't.
Jesus: Truly, no one who has left home, family, fields for me and the gospel...

Will fail to receive a hundred times as much in this present age (homes, family, fields, along with persecutions) and in the age to come eternal life. Present and future reward. But persecutions included. Kingdom life brings both abundance and suffering.
Many who are first will be last, and the last first. Kingdom inversion. The rich man (first) is last; the disciples (last) are first. Reversal of earthly status.
On the way up to Jerusalem, Jesus leading, disciples astonished, followers afraid. Ominous journey. Jesus' determination, their dread. He takes the Twelve aside.

We are going up to Jerusalem. The Son of Man will be delivered to chief priests and teachers. They will condemn him to death, hand him over to Gentiles. Third passion prediction. Most detailed yet. Betrayal, Jewish trial, Gentile execution.
They will mock, spit on, flog, kill him. Three days later he will rise. Graphic specifics. But the resurrection promise stands.
James and John: Teacher, we want you to do whatever we ask. Audacious. Blank check request. Immediately after the passion prediction.
What do you want me to do? Jesus asks, knowing already.
Let one of us sit at your right and the other at your left in your glory. Positions of honor. They still expect an earthly throne. Glory without suffering.

You don't know what you are asking. Can you drink the cup I drink or be baptized with the baptism I am baptized with? Cup = suffering (14:36, Isaiah 51:17). Baptism = overwhelming ordeal. They request glory; he offers suffering.
We can. Confident, ignorant. Jesus: You will drink the cup and be baptized with the baptism. Prophetic. James martyred (Acts 12:2), John exiled, suffered.
But to sit at my right or left is not for me to grant. These places belong to those for whom they have been prepared. The Father determines positions. Humility before divine sovereignty.
The ten heard, became indignant with James and John. Not righteous indignation, but jealousy. They wanted the same.
Jesus calls them together. Gentile rulers lord it over people, high officials exercise authority. Domination is the world's way.
Not so with you. Whoever wants to be great must be your servant. Kingdom greatness is servanthood. Inverted hierarchy.
Whoever wants to be first must be slave of all. Lowest position = highest rank. Radical redefinition.

Even the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many. The ultimate example. Jesus models what he teaches. Ransom (lutron, λύτρον): payment to free captives. Substitutionary atonement. His life for many. Isaiah 53.
They come to Jericho. As they leave, a blind man, Bartimaeus (son of Timaeus), sits by the roadside begging. Named. Memorable encounter.
He hears it is Jesus of Nazareth, shouts: Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me! Messianic title. He confesses what the disciples struggle to understand. Blind man sees what seeing men miss.
Many rebuke him, tell him to be quiet. He shouts all the more: Son of David, have mercy! Persistent faith. He will not be silenced.
Jesus stops. Call him. They call: Cheer up! On your feet! He's calling you. Jesus honors desperate faith.
Throwing his cloak aside, he jumps up, comes to Jesus. Abandon everything. Cloak = only possession. He leaves it behind.
What do you want me to do for you? Same question to Bartimaeus as to James and John (v. 36). Bartimaeus knows what he needs: Rabbi, I want to see.

Go, your faith has healed you. Immediately he receives sight and follows Jesus along the road. Sight leads to discipleship. He sees physically and spiritually. He follows Jesus to Jerusalem, to the cross. The chapter ends with a blind man seeing. The disciples, still blind, follow Jesus to his passion. Chapter ten: marriage, children, wealth, ambition, blindness. All themes converge on the journey to Jerusalem. The Son of Man goes to serve and to die.
